notes: All claims verified: 1909-1986, clarinetist/bandleader, 'King of Swing', January 16, 1938 Carnegie Hall concert described as 'the single most important jazz or popular music concert in history', and 'led one of the first integrated jazz groups, his trio and quartet'.

Dates (1929-1998), vocals, and improvisation/scatting claims verified. Two substantive claims cannot be checked: 'She ran her own label for years' and 'mentored countless young players through her working bands' -- the extract truncates around 1961, before either topic. Background knowledge says both are true (Bet-Car Records, her bands as a training ground), but that is not citable here; no contradiction found.

wrongClaims.problem: Celebration was released in 1991 on World Circuit, not 1992 on Verve; Verve signed him only after Celebration
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: His 1991 album Celebration, which featured Courtney Pine among a number of British players as guests, was nominated for a Mercury Music Prize. After this, Mseleku was taken up by Verve Records for several albums.

wrongClaims.problem: discography confirms label and year: World Circuit, 1991

wrongClaims.ourClaim: featured Joe Henderson and Pharoah Sanders
wrongClaims.problem: per evidence, Celebration's guests were Courtney Pine and British players; Joe Henderson appeared on the first of the later Verve albums, not on Celebration; Pharoah Sanders is not mentioned anywhere in the evidence
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: The first of these featured a number of American players, including Joe Henderson, Abbey Lincoln and Elvin Jones.

notes: Right entity (1955-2008, South African, pianist/saxophonist, self-taught, spiritual themes all match), but the album sentence conflates Celebration (World Circuit, 1991, Courtney Pine) with his subsequent Verve recordings (Joe Henderson et al.). 'Struggles with health and addiction': health supported (diabetic, bipolar diagnosis); addiction not mentioned in evidence. Repair should rewrite the Celebration sentence.

Core skeleton fully verified: 1929-1980, piano, Kind of Blue with Miles Davis (modal jazz), trio with Scott LaFaro and Paul Motian, LaFaro's death in a 1961 car crash. Unverifiable claims: 'struggles with drug addiction' (extract truncates before this; widely documented but not citable here); influence specifically on 'Keith Jarrett, Brad Mehldau, Fred Hersch' (evidence says only 'continue to influence jazz pianists today'). 'Debussy and Ravel' maps plausibly to evidence's 'impressionist harmony' but the names are not in the extract. No contradictions.
Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead (ECM 1980s, Downtown Scene, John Zorn, Paul Motian's groups until Motian's death in 2011). Born March 18, 1951; alive (deathYear null correct).

Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead; 1915-1959 verified. Data-quality flag (not a contradiction): instruments field is ['musician'], a non-instrument placeholder -- evidence supports 'vocals' ('American jazz singer'). Recommend field cleanup.

All claims verified: born 1944 in Colon, Panama, raised in Brooklyn; Horace Silver then Miles Davis before co-founding Mahavishnu Orchestra with John McLaughlin (1971); Birds of Fire (1973) and solo debut Spectrum (1973); influence on rock/metal drummers (Bill Bruford, Danny Carey of Tool); multiple bass drums and early electric drum kit support the double-bass/electronic claims.

Verified: born 1943, tenor saxophonist, Coltrane-influenced ('one of a generation of Coltrane-influenced tenor saxophonists'), Lee Morgan sideman (The Last Session; present at Morgan's death per I Called Him Morgan), formed his own group in 1979, spiritual-jazz framing supported by the Capra Black description ('profoundly spiritual effort').
Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead. Born 4 September 1966; alive; guitar verified.
Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead (Riverside/Mainstream/Blue Note, most recorded trumpeter with jazz organ, 27 sideman recordings). 1930-1979 verified.

Verified: born 1950, flute, first female instrumentalist signed by Blue Note (1971), Blacks and Blues with the Mizell Brothers, jazz/funk/soul blend. One substantive claim exceeds evidence scope: ours says the album 'became one of Blue Note's biggest sellers' (label-wide), while evidence says it 'was one of her biggest selling albums for Blue Note' (artist-scoped, peaked no. 84 Billboard 200). Not a contradiction, but the label-wide superlative cannot be checked; repair could rescope to match evidence.

Verified: 1941-2016, vibraphone and marimba, 1960s Blue Note work with Jackie McLean (One Step Beyond, his first Blue Note date), Eric Dolphy, and Andrew Hill. Avant-garde-plus-lyricism framing is editorial and consistent with the post-bop/free jazz session history.

Bio is essentially verbatim the Wikipedia lead (two Jazz Messengers stints, Cannonball Adderley in between, 'Moanin''/'Dat Dere'/'This Here', early-mid 1960s trios). 1935-1974 verified. Minor field note: instruments lists 'composer' (a role, not an instrument) alongside piano; both supported by 'American jazz pianist and composer'.

Verified: born 1944, Swedish pianist, long-running trio, deep ECM association (many ECM titles in discography), worked with Jan Garbarek (co-led Witchi-Tai-To and Dansere) and joined Charles Lloyd's quartet in 1988 (five Lloyd ECM albums listed). 'Nordic aesthetics' language is editorial.
Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead (Max Roach, John Coltrane, Eric Dolphy; influenced by Sonny Rollins and Clifford Brown; died aged 23). 1938-1961 verified.

Verified: born 1970, trio since early 1990s, Art of the Trio series praised, pop/rock/classical absorption, simultaneous melodies in separate hands influencing other musicians, Joshua Redman quartet mid-1990s, Pat Metheny collaboration, solo piano formats. Unverified illustrative examples: 'Radiohead' and 'Nick Drake' are not named in the extract (the underlying claim -- pop interpretations expanding repertoire -- is directly supported). Note: evidence complicates the 'Bill Evans's lyricism' framing (critics reassessed the Evans comparison), but ours phrases it as editorial interpretation, not flagged.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: Branford Marsalis (born August 26, 1960) is an American saxophonist, composer, and bandleader.

notes: Bio prose is verbatim the Wikipedia lead and fully verified (Branford Marsalis Quartet, classical soloist work, Buckshot LeFonque, Tonight Show Band 1992-1995); born 1960, alive. The defect is the instruments field: it lists only the roles 'composer' and 'bandleader' and omits his actual instrument, saxophone -- consistent with a parse artifact that dropped the first token of 'saxophonist, composer, and bandleader'. Repair: set instruments to saxophone (he plays soprano and tenor per the extract).

Verified: 'Brother' Jack McDuff, 1926-2001, organist, soul jazz era prominence, gave George Benson his first break (young Benson in his groups), 1960s recordings on Prestige and then Atlantic. Minor: 'Hammond B-3' model is not specified in the extract (evidence says only 'organist'); standard shorthand, not flagged as substantive.

Verified: 1924-1966 (evidence explicitly notes the 1922 birth-certificate date was incorrect; ours matches the correct 1924), 1945 police beating followed by institutionalizations, attacking right-hand approach breaking from stride's left-hand style, late-1940s/early-1950s recordings with Parker/Stitt/Roach important to modern jazz piano. 'Influencing every jazz pianist from Bill Evans to Herbie Hancock' is editorial hyperbole; evidence names Walter Davis Jr., Toshiko Akiyoshi, Barry Harris instead -- not contradicted, not flagged.
Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead (cornetist, key figure in New Orleans ragtime/'jass' becoming jazz). 1877-1931 verified.

Verified: 1904-1991, Texas-born alto saxophonist and clarinetist, mentor to the young Charlie Parker, Kansas City-area scene via the Blue Devils, only leader album The Legendary Buster Smith (Atlantic, 1959). Unverifiable: 'co-wrote One O'Clock Jump with Count Basie' -- the tune is never mentioned in the evidence; this credit is historically contested (often officially credited to Basie alone), so it needs a real source before shipping. Also 'guitarist' in instruments: evidence shows only bass guitar, taken up after his 1960s auto accident; his father was the guitarist. No contradictions.

Verified: 1925-1982, vibraphone plus drums/bongos/congas/timbales (percussion), Dave Brubeck Octet/Trio and George Shearing sideman, own Latin combos from 1954 to his death (~three decades), prolific on Fantasy and Verve, Soul Sauce (1964) his biggest success. HIGH-PRIORITY REVIEW: ours says Soul Sauce 'won a Grammy' -- evidence instead says 'His Grammy Award in 1980 for his album La Onda Va Bien capped off a career', strongly implying his Grammy was for La Onda Va Bien, not Soul Sauce; background knowledge agrees Tjader's only Grammy was 1980 La Onda Va Bien. The extract truncates mid-discussion of Soul Sauce and never explicitly denies it a Grammy, so per the rules this is unverifiable, not factual_error -- but repair should treat the Grammy attribution as almost certainly wrong. 'One of the best-selling Latin jazz albums ever recorded' also unverifiable (evidence only artist-scoped).

Verified: 1928-1975, alto sax, moved from Florida to New York in 1955, hailed as heir to Charlie Parker's mantle ('new Bird'), blues-rooted style, Kind of Blue with Miles Davis, quintet with brother Nat, 'Mercy, Mercy, Mercy' (1966 soul jazz hit), died at 46 after a stroke. Minor unverified color: 'hosting educational concerts and TV programs' -- educator role supported (narrated The Child's Introduction to Jazz, 1961), but TV hosting specifically is not in the extract.

Verified: 1936-2023, composer/pianist/organist/bandleader, jazz opera Escalator over the Hill (triple LP), co-led the Jazz Composers' Orchestra and started its JCOA label ('helped found' consistent), big band work, compositions performed by many artists. Minor unverified: 'self-taught' -- evidence says her piano-teacher father encouraged her to learn piano and does not address her training as a composer; commonly described as self-taught as a composer, but not checkable here. Not contradicted.

Verified: 1920-1994, vocals and piano (early jobs as pianist with Benny Carter's band and at Minton's), Billie Holiday as primary influence ('disciple' supported; always sang at least one Lady Day song), developed her own style (Chicago years), behind-the-beat phrasing and ironic lyric interpretation support the diction/phrasing/wit description. Minor unverified color: 'her between-song commentary became legendary' is not in the extract; reputational flourish, not contradicted.
